meaning: adj.[鍖籡鑵硅偂娌熻吅鐐庣殑
examples: In 1616-1619 an epidemic, possibly of bubonic or pneumonic plague, swept coastal New England, killing as many as nine out of ten.
Take bubonic plague for example. The disease is most commonly transmitted to humans through flea bites, and the fleas live on rodents.
They also ruled out bubonic plague or tuberculosis.
In 14th century Europe, the bubonic plague temporarily boosted wages and reduced agricultural rents.
Without a groundswell of rodents, fears of bubonic plague should be set aside, or never feared at all.
